title: Proof and Canonicality
subtitle: A starting shape for model, parser, protocol, or truth-claim work where assertions, findings, and proof strength matter.
scope: A domain where formal claims, staged proof, findings, and decisions need to be kept distinct but connected.
sourceCoverage: Template only; replace placeholder references with real formal primitives, invariants, evidence, tickets, and tooling.

templateMeta.useWhen: Use this when the work is about defending a truth claim and keeping model, evidence, findings, decisions, and tooling distinct.

objective: Make a truth-bearing domain legible by separating the model, the assertion, the proof ladder, the findings, the decisions, and the tooling.
successCondition: A reader can tell what is being claimed, what proof exists, where evidence is weak, what was learned, and which tools are used to evolve or verify the claim.

callouts.title: How to use this template
items: Use this when the work is about proving or defending a model truth, not just implementing a feature.
Keep assertions, findings, and decisions in separate sections so the document does not collapse claim and evidence into one blur.

callout.title: Template intent
items: This template is optimized for canonicality, model integrity, and staged proof growth.
If the work becomes broad implementation tracking, add a separate capability or operating surface rather than stretching the proof sections.

data.name: Authoritative graph semantics
data.definition: The model must preserve graph-valid relationships through every evolution pass.
data.authorityScope: Any parser or repair path that claims semantic correctness for the graph representation.
notes: Use this section when several model elements need to be held together in parallel.
violations: Placeholder example: runtime can parse locally but still fail to represent the authoritative edge semantics.

data.name: Canonical truth path
notes: Use this section for the singular claim the rest of the proof structure is trying to support.

data.name: Frozen evidence replay
notes: Start with the smallest stable evidence rung that can be rerun without interpretation drift.

data.name: Semantic equivalence checks
notes: Each rung should increase proof strength rather than merely adding another noisy test.

data.name: Production drift detection
notes: Reserve upper rungs for evidence that is closer to operational truth.

data.name: Generic runtime lacks required edge semantics
notes: Keep findings factual. They should describe what was observed, not what should be done next.

data.name: Local green tests do not imply canonical model conformance
notes: Use `reference` when a finding is informative but not itself the central authoritative truth.

data.name: Prefer graph-valid fixes before parser heuristics
notes: Decision sections capture operating truth. They are not merely observations.

data.name: Proof replay runner
data.purpose: Replay retained evidence against the current implementation so each proof rung can be evaluated without fresh interpretation noise.
data.whenToUse: Use when a claim needs replayable evidence rather than one-off local confirmation.
notes: Replace placeholder script and artifact paths with your real proof harness.
caveats: A replay harness can still be insufficient if the runtime cannot express the semantics the model requires.

data.name: Living-doc renderer for proof ladders
data.purpose: Package the current proof state into a portable snapshot with lineage so claims, findings, and decisions can be reviewed together.
data.whenToUse: Use when the proof structure needs to be shared without requiring access to every underlying system.
caveats: The rendered HTML preserves snapshot-relative time, but it is still a point-in-time artifact.
